name: Fred Waring
abstract: Fredrick Malcolm Waring Sr. (June 9, 1900 – July 29, 1984) was an American musician, bandleader, choral director, and radio and television personality, sometimes referred to as "America's Singing Master" and "The Man Who Taught America How to Sing".

concerts.label: Pleasure Time National College Glee Club Competition
Pleasure Time National College Glee Club Competition
Harvest Song Festival and New York State Choral Contest

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee does not mention Fred Waring; the mention is made solely by the interviewer in the context of a third party's story.

about_quotes.speaker: Tony Marino
about_quotes.evidence: He wrote a lot of music for Fred Waring who was across the street, the Pennsylvanian.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tony Marino interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, August 25, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: Well, this is the thing that Fred Waring fought for years and got nowhere... He had, oh, like seven or eight wives with no divorces, and he used to steal the Western Union boys' bicycles, and he was a thoroughly crazy man, but very talented.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Maxwell
about_quotes.evidence: I think, though, that the downfall of everything started with recordings. In the '20s and '30s, people like Fred Waring and a lot of other people fought commercial use of recordings...

about_quotes.speaker: Godfrey Hirsch
about_quotes.evidence: Wade was a, Wade was a, there was another trumpet player... he was with Waring quite a while. With Fred Waring, he was strictly a, he was strictly a gag man.

about_quotes.speaker: William T. (Bill) Challis
about_quotes.evidence: The Waring Banjo Orchestra. Fred Waring. Well Tom had it then. Fred was still at school while I was there. He was at Penn State which was 60 miles away.
